skill.name: web-security-testing
skill.description: A structured workflow for authorized web application security testing across OWASP Top 10 risk areas.
skill.summary: Guides authorized OWASP Top 10 web application security assessments.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static external-command findings are false positives caused by Markdown backticks around skill names and copy-paste prompts, not executable Ruby or shell code. The weak-cryptography findings are also false positives because the cited lines describe OWASP categories and do not name or implement cryptographic algorithms. The remaining concern is dual-use security testing guidance that includes injection, brute-force, and credential-stuffing test areas, so publication should include an authorization warning.

medium_findings.title: Dual-use Web Security Testing Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill is intended for penetration testing and bug bounty work and includes testing for injection, command injection, credential stuffing, brute force protection, IDOR, and path traversal. These are legitimate when authorized, but the guidance could be misused against systems without permission.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The workflow explicitly covers penetration testing and bug bounty contexts and lists offensive test categories. It does not provide exploit code, so the issue is dual-use risk rather than confirmed malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged Markdown backticks and fenced prompt blocks as Ruby or shell execution. These lines contain skill names, checklist text, and copy-paste prompts, with no executable Ruby code or shell command invocation in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited evidence is Markdown content, not source code. I found no shell execution syntax, scripts, or command runner implementation in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Pattern Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography alerts do not correspond to an implemented algorithm or insecure cryptographic API. The cited lines describe the web security workflow and OWASP checklist topics, not cryptographic code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain descriptive Markdown text and no cryptographic primitive. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, ECB mode, or similar weak algorithm usage in the reviewed file.

content.value_statement: Web security reviews need consistent coverage across common vulnerability classes. This skill organizes authorized testing around OWASP Top 10 areas, supporting repeatable assessment and reporting.
actual_capabilities: Organizes web application testing into reconnaissance, injection, XSS, authentication, access control, header, and reporting phases.
Lists related specialist skills to invoke for focused vulnerability testing workflows.
Provides copy-paste prompts for common OWASP Top 10 testing phases.
Includes a checklist for OWASP Top 10 coverage and final quality gates.
Supports security report preparation with risk assessment and remediation steps.
limitations: It is a workflow guide, not an automated scanner or exploit tool.
It does not verify authorization or target ownership by itself.
It depends on other referenced skills for detailed testing procedures.
It does not include environment-specific legal, compliance, or scope controls.

use_cases.title: Plan an Authorized Web App Assessment
use_cases.description: Build a phase-based test plan that covers reconnaissance, vulnerability classes, and reporting gates.

use_cases.title: Review OWASP Top 10 Coverage
use_cases.description: Check whether a web security review includes major OWASP categories before final reporting.

use_cases.title: Structure Bug Bounty Notes
use_cases.description: Organize findings, proof-of-concept notes, risk ratings, and remediation guidance for permitted programs.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Test Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for an authorized assessment.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an authorized web application security test plan for this target scope. Include phases, OWASP Top 10 coverage, evidence to collect, and reporting gates.

prompt_templates.title: Check OWASP Coverage
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate review of an existing assessment plan.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my web application testing checklist against OWASP Top 10. Identify missing categories, weak evidence requirements, and unclear authorization boundaries.

prompt_templates.title: Prioritize Findings
prompt_templates.prompt: Group these web security findings by risk, affected workflow, likely impact, and remediation priority. Flag items that need stronger proof before reporting.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Executive Report
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert these authorized web application security findings into an executive report with scope, methodology, risk summary, business impact, and remediation roadmap.

output_examples.input: A team needs to assess a customer portal before launch.
output: A phased plan covering reconnaissance, injection, XSS, authentication, access control, security headers, and final reporting.
A checklist of required evidence for each vulnerability class.
A reporting structure with risk ratings and remediation owners.

output_examples.input: A tester has notes from an authorized bug bounty review.
output: Findings grouped by affected feature and OWASP category.
Items that need additional proof before submission.
Clear remediation guidance for each confirmed issue.

output_examples.input: An application security lead wants a final coverage check.
output: A gap list for untested OWASP Top 10 areas.
Recommended follow-up tests for authentication and access control.
Quality gates to complete before the report is delivered.

best_practices: Use this skill only for systems where you have explicit written authorization.
Define scope, rate limits, test accounts, and prohibited actions before testing begins.
Record evidence, impact, reproduction steps, and remediation guidance for every confirmed finding.
anti_patterns: Do not use the workflow against third-party systems without permission.
Do not run brute-force, credential-stuffing, or automated scanning outside the approved scope.
Do not report speculative issues without evidence, impact, and clear reproduction steps.

faq.question: Does this skill run security scans automatically?
faq.answer: No. It is a workflow guide that helps organize authorized testing and reporting.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a warning. It is dual-use security guidance and should be limited to authorized testing.

faq.question: Which security areas does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers reconnaissance, injection, XSS, authentication, access control, security headers, and reporting.

faq.question: Does it include exploit code?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed file contains phase guidance, referenced skills, prompts, and checklists.

faq.question: Can it replace a professional penetration test?
faq.answer: No. It helps structure work, but qualified testers must validate findings and legal scope.

faq.question: What should users prepare before using it?
faq.answer: Users should prepare written authorization, target scope, testing limits, credentials, and reporting requirements.
